era_context: The early 7th century CE continues with the Byzantine-Sasanid War in its sixth year (602–628 CE). The Byzantine Empire under Emperor Phocas (r. 602–610 CE) was experiencing severe military and financial strain. The Sasanid Empire under Khosrau II was expanding its territorial control in the Levant and Mesopotamia. In China, the Sui Dynasty (581–618 CE) was consolidating control. The Arabian Peninsula remained fragmented among various Arab tribes and merchant communities, with Mecca and Medina as important commercial centers.

events.title: Continued Byzantine-Sasanid military conflict
events.description: The Byzantine-Sasanid War continued with Sasanid forces maintaining military pressure on Byzantine territories. The year 607 CE falls within the period of Sasanid military dominance, when Khosrau II's forces were systematically conquering Byzantine-held lands in the Levant and Mesopotamia. The specific military operations of 607 CE are poorly documented in surviving sources, but the broader context is clear: the Sasanid Empire was at the height of its territorial expansion, and the Byzantine Empire was on the defensive. This period of Sasanid dominance would last until Heraclius's military campaigns in the 620s CE.

sources.name: Theophylact Simocatta, History (early 7th century CE, contemporary chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of the Byzantine-Sasanid War is well-attested, but specific events in 607 CE are not documented in surviving sources. The dating is approximate within ±2–3 years. This entry reflects the general historical context rather than documented events for this specific year.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for 607 CE in surviving sources. The historical record for this year is essentially blank, which may reflect gaps in documentation rather than the absence of significant events. The broader Byzantine-Sasanid War is well-attested, but this particular year is not marked by any recorded major events.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 607 CE is minimal to negligible. Contemporary sources provide general context about the Byzantine-Sasanid War but do not document specific events for this year. This reflects the uneven nature of historical documentation in the early 7th century CE: major events are recorded, but many years pass with little or no documentation. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward Byzantine and Syriac Christian perspectives. Arabian Peninsula events are almost entirely absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: Part of the broader Sasanid military campaign that culminated in the capture of Jerusalem in 614 CE
